title: Antigone: The Ode to Man
description: The chorus of Antigone sings the 'Ode to Man' — humanity as the most wondrous and terrible creature.
introduction: Sophocles' Antigone stages the collision between human law and divine law. When Creon forbids the burial of her brother Polyneices, Antigone defies him in the name of the 'unwritten laws' that transcend any ruler's decree. The play's choral ode — polla ta deina, 'many are the wonders' — is the most celebrated meditation on human nature in all of Greek literature: humanity as simultaneously magnificent and monstrous, capable of mastering nature yet unable to escape death.

translation.original_text: πολλὰ τὰ δεινὰ κοὐδὲν ἀνθρώπου δεινότερον πέλει. τοῦτο καὶ πολιοῦ πέραν πόντου χειμερίῳ νότῳ χωρεῖ, περιβρυχίοισιν περῶν ὑπ᾿ οἴδμασιν.
translation.text: Many are the wonders, and nothing is more wondrous than man. This creature crosses the grey sea in the winter storm, passing through the surging waves.

translation.original_text: οὐ γάρ τί μοι Ζεὺς ἦν ὁ κηρύξας τάδε, οὐδ᾿ ἡ ξύνοικος τῶν κάτω θεῶν Δίκη... οὐδὲ σθένειν τοσοῦτον ᾠόμην τὰ σὰ κηρύγμαθ᾿ ὥστ᾿ ἄγραπτα κἀσφαλῆ θεῶν νόμιμα δύνασθαί θνητὸν ὄνθ᾿ ὑπερδραμεῖν.
translation.text: It was not Zeus who proclaimed this to me, nor Justice who dwells with the gods below... nor did I think your decrees were of such force that a mortal could override the unwritten and unfailing laws of the gods.
